title: 2026 Big Ten Basketball Was More than Elite
text:
Every March, the same debate controls the college sports scene, which conference is truly the best? The Big 12 consistently has a challenging regular season, and the SEC prides itself on having top tier athletes, but these narratives don’t win championships. When you take away the loud, often misleading, voices and take a look at the numbers from the 2025-2026 season, reality shows its true colors. The Big Ten didn’t just participate in this historic season, they dominated it.
The Big Ten set a completely new standard this year, proving they were better than the SEC, the Big 12, and everyone else contending for the title.
The Non-Conference Start: Winning by Margins


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(hoopR)
library(patchwork)
library(ggrepel)


# downloaded hoopR information to make it easier when running the code chunk over time
bball_data <- read_csv("data/ncaa_mbb.csv")


# get the conference data to join, data from sports reference
conference <- read_csv("data/teams_conferences.csv")


# select only what I need
conference <- conference |>
  select(School, Conf, SOS)


# join bball with conference
bball_data <- bball_data |>
  left_join(conference, by = c("team_location" = "School"))


# filter for non conference games
non_conf_bball <- bball_data |>
  #filter for games before conference play since conference play differentials won't work
  filter(game_date < as.Date("2026-01-01"))


# time for the actual first chart I have
chart1_data <- non_conf_bball |>
  left_join(conference, by = c("opponent_team_location" = "School"), suffix = c("", "_opp")) |>
  # filter to ensure only non conference games are considered
  filter(Conf != Conf_opp | is.na(Conf_opp)) |>
  mutate(
    score_diff = team_score - opponent_team_score,
    date_of_game = as.Date(game_date),
  ) |>
  group_by(Conf, date_of_game) |>
  summarize(
    avg_score_diff = mean(score_diff, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


chart1_data_bigten <- chart1_data |>
  filter(Conf == "Big Ten")

chart1_data_sec <- chart1_data |>
  filter(Conf == "SEC")

chart1_data_bigeast <- chart1_data |>
  filter(Conf == "Big East")


ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = chart1_data,
    aes(x = date_of_game, y = avg_score_diff, group = Conf),
    color = "grey",
    alpha = 0.5,
    linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(
    data = chart1_data_sec,
    aes(x = date_of_game, y = avg_score_diff, group = Conf),
    color = "red3",
    alpha = 0.2,
    linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_line(
    data = chart1_data_bigeast,
    aes(x = date_of_game, y = avg_score_diff, group = Conf),
    color = "navy",
    alpha = 0.2,
    linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_line(
    data = chart1_data_bigten,
    aes(x = date_of_game, y = avg_score_diff, group = Conf),
    color = "deepskyblue3",
    linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black") +
  annotate(
    "text", x = as.Date("2025-12-12"), y = 33, label = "Big Ten", color = "deepskyblue3", fontface = "bold", size = 4
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = as.Date("2025-12-07"), y = -37, label = "SEC", color = "red3", fontface = "bold", size = 4
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text", x = as.Date("2025-11-25"), y = -32, label = "Big East", color = "navy", fontface = "bold", size = 4
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "The Big Ten consistently outscored opponents",
    subtitle = "Among all conferences, the B1G stayed steadily above the average for point differential.",
    x = "Game Date",
    y = "Score Differentials",
    caption = "By: Jake Ostdiek | Source: SportsDataverse for R & SportsReference"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )









To find the true strength of a conference, you have to first look at the non-conference competition that starts off every season. As soon as January hits, conference rivals go at it, dragging down overall conference metrics. But in the non-conference slate? That is where you see what each conference is really made of.
When we look at all games played before January 1st, 2026 (Average start date for conference games), the Big Ten teases its superiority.
The data reveals a consistent trend. The Big Ten wasn’t just winning games, they were tearing through defenses and locking down offenses. Looking at the average scoring differentials, the Big Ten separated itself from the pack early on. While other conferences hovered near the baseline of “just okay”, the Big Ten consistently was outscoring their opponents by significant margins. This established a level of dominance before conference play was even a worry.
The Test of Resilience: Strength of Schedule


Code
# find number of teams per conference
conf_teams <- conference |>
  group_by(Conf) |>
  summarize(
    num_teams = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


# chart 2 is strength of schedule for the top 10 conferences
chart2_data <- non_conf_bball |>
  # group_by summarise to average each conferences SOS
  group_by(Conf) |>
  summarize(
    avg_conf_sos = mean(SOS, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(conf_teams, by = "Conf") |>
  arrange(desc(avg_conf_sos)) |>
  head(10)


chart2_data_bigten <- chart2_data |>
  filter(Conf == "Big Ten")

chart2_data_sec <- chart2_data |>
  filter(Conf == "SEC")

chart2_data_bigeast <- chart2_data |>
  filter(Conf == "Big East")


ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = chart2_data,
    aes(x = avg_conf_sos, y = reorder(Conf, avg_conf_sos), size = num_teams),
    color="grey",
    alpha=.5) +
  geom_point(
    data = chart2_data_sec,
    aes(x = avg_conf_sos, y = reorder(Conf, avg_conf_sos), size = num_teams),
    color = "red3",
    alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(
    data = chart2_data_bigeast,
    aes(x = avg_conf_sos, y = reorder(Conf, avg_conf_sos), size = num_teams),
    color = "navy",
    alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(
    data = chart2_data_bigten,
    aes(x = avg_conf_sos, y = reorder(Conf, avg_conf_sos), size = num_teams),
    color="deepskyblue3",
    alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(5, 10)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(-1, 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 13)) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=chart2_data,
    aes(x = avg_conf_sos, y = reorder(Conf, avg_conf_sos), label = Conf),
    color = "black",
    hjust = 0) +
  annotate("rect", fill="blue", alpha=0.1,
           xmin=8.1, xmax=Inf,
           ymin=5, ymax=Inf) +
  theme_minimal() +
  geom_text(
    aes(x=10.7, y=5.8, label="Tough Schedules"), size=3, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_text(
    aes(x=3.3, y=8, label="B1G had the toughest\nstrength of schedule (11.38)", size = 4, fontface = "bold")) +
  geom_text(
    aes(x=9, y=3, label="The SEC and Big 12 are close,\nbut have two fewer schools", size = 4, fontface = "bold")) +
  labs(
    title="The Big Ten Leads in Schedule Difficulty",
    subtitle="Compared to other major conferences, the B1G had the most difficult path to the postseason.\n(Point size = number of schools in conference).",
    x="Average Strength of Schedule (SOS)",
    y="Conference",
    caption = "By: Jake Ostdiek | Source: SportsDataverse for R & SportsReference"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )









The immediate counterargument for non-conference blowouts usually sounds like this, “Oh yeah, name one tough opponent you’ve played.” That’s fair to ask of someone prideful in their conference. Padding the stat sheet against mid-majors before the new year has become something of a college basketball tradition.
But the Big Ten wasn’t hiding from anyone.
When mapping the average Strength of Schedule (SOS) across the top conferences, the Big Ten stands its ground at the top. They didn’t just rack up high point differentials against non-conference teams, they did it against the toughest competition in the country as well. This wasn’t made possible by a few outstanding teams carrying the rest of the conference either. From top to bottom, the conference was tough enough to push through, ensuring any team that came out of the Big Ten regular season was battle-tested and ready for a fight.
The SEC and Big 12 would argue they are right up there with the Big Ten, but in reality, those two conferences both have 16 teams while the Big Ten has 18. With two additional teams, a strong average strength of schedule becomes even tougher to maintain. With more teams, there will be more competition, and where some conferences struggle with their level of competition, the Big Ten took it in stride to continue elevating their performance.
The Ultimate Test: March Madness
Regular season metrics are great, but the NCAA Tournament is a lie detector test disguised as a college basketball tournament. If a conference is truly the best, it has to be able to continue their regular-season hype into notable tournament success.
To measure this, we don’t just look at how many bids a conference received, we look at efficiency. How successfully did a conference’s total number of teams survive from round to round?


Code
# chart 3 will show the big ten's success in the ncaa tournament with a horizontal bar chart
# Luckily I already had these teams in listed in a R project I did for another class predicting march madness with ML
ncaa_tourney_teams <- c(
  #East
  "Duke", "Siena", "Ohio State", "TCU", "St. John's", "Northern Iowa", "Kansas", "California Baptist", "Louisville", "South Florida", "Michigan State", "North Dakota State", "UCLA", "UCF", "UConn", "Furman",
  
  # Midwest
  "Michigan", "Howard", "Georgia", "Saint Louis", "Texas Tech", "Akron", "Alabama", "Hofstra", "Tennessee", "Miami (OH)", "Virginia", "Wright State", "Kentucky", "Santa Clara", "Iowa State", "Tennessee State",
  
  # South
  "Florida", "Prairie View A&M", "Clemson", "Iowa", "Vanderbilt", "McNeese", "Nebraska", "Troy", "North Carolina", "VCU", "Illinois", "Pennsylvania", "Saint Mary's", "Texas A&M", "Houston", "Idaho",
  
  # West
  "Arizona", "Long Island University", "Villanova", "Utah State", "Wisconsin", "High Point", "Arkansas", "Hawai'i", "BYU", "Texas", "Gonzaga", "Kennesaw State", "Miami", "Missouri", "Purdue", "Queens University")


tourney_data <- bball_data |>
  filter(
    season_type == 3
  ) |>
  filter(
    team_location %in% ncaa_tourney_teams
  )


# round of 64 teams
r64_data <- tourney_data |>
  distinct(Conf, team_location) |>
  group_by(Conf) |>
  summarise(
    r64_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


# round of 32 by filtering game dates
r32_data <- tourney_data |>
  filter(
    game_date >= as.Date("2026-03-21") & game_date <= ("2026-03-22")
  ) |>
  distinct(Conf, team_location) |>
  group_by(Conf) |>
  summarise(
    r32_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


# sweet 16 by filtering game dates
s16_data <- tourney_data |>
  filter(
    game_date >= as.Date("2026-03-26") & game_date <= ("2026-03-27")
  ) |>
  distinct(Conf, team_location) |>
  group_by(Conf) |>
  summarise(
    s16_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


# elite 8 same thing
e8_data <- tourney_data |>
  filter(
    game_date >= as.Date("2026-03-28") & game_date <= ("2026-03-29")
  ) |>
  distinct(Conf, team_location) |>
  group_by(Conf) |>
  summarise(
    e8_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


# final 4 same thing
f4_data <- tourney_data |>
  filter(
    game_date == as.Date("2026-04-04")
  ) |>
  distinct(Conf, team_location) |>
  group_by(Conf) |>
  summarise(
    f4_count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


# Michigan won it all so even better for the purpose of this post

# I am going to find the conversion rates from round to round to see conference success

conversion_data <- r64_data |>
  left_join(r32_data, by ="Conf") |>
  left_join(s16_data, by ="Conf") |>
  left_join(e8_data, by ="Conf") |>
  left_join(f4_data, by ="Conf") |>
  mutate(
    conv_64_32 = r32_count / r64_count * 100,
    conv_32_16 = s16_count / r32_count * 100,
    conv_16_8 = e8_count / s16_count * 100,
    conv_8_4 = f4_count / e8_count * 100
  )


conversion_data_bigten <- conversion_data |>
  filter(Conf == "Big Ten")


conv1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_64_32),
    aes(x = r32_count, y = reorder(Conf, r32_count)), 
    fill = "cornsilk4",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data_bigten |> drop_na(conv_64_32),
    aes(x = r32_count, y = reorder(Conf, r32_count)),
    fill = "deepskyblue3",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_64_32),
    aes(x = r32_count, y = reorder(Conf, r32_count),
    label = paste0(round(conv_64_32, 1), "%")), hjust = 1.2, color = "white", size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Round of 64 to Round of 32",
    x = "# of Teams Advanced",
    y = "") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )


conv2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_32_16),
    aes(x = s16_count, y = reorder(Conf, s16_count)), 
    fill = "cornsilk4",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data_bigten |> drop_na(conv_32_16),
    aes(x = s16_count, y = reorder(Conf, s16_count)),
    fill = "deepskyblue3",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_32_16),
    aes(x = s16_count, y = reorder(Conf, s16_count),
    label = paste0(round(conv_32_16, 1), "%")), hjust = 1.2, color = "white") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Round of 32 to Sweet 16",
    x = "# of Teams Advanced",
    y = "") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )


conv3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_16_8),
    aes(x = e8_count, y = reorder(Conf, e8_count)), 
    fill = "cornsilk4",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data_bigten |> drop_na(conv_16_8),
    aes(x = e8_count, y = reorder(Conf, e8_count)),
    fill = "deepskyblue3",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_16_8),
    aes(x = e8_count, y = reorder(Conf, e8_count),
    label = paste0(round(conv_16_8, 1), "%")), hjust = 1.2, color = "white") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Sweet 16 to Elite Eight",
    x = "# of Teams Advanced",
    y = "") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )


conv4 <- ggplot() +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_8_4),
    aes(x = f4_count, y = reorder(Conf, f4_count)),
    fill = "cornsilk4",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_col(
    data = conversion_data_bigten |> drop_na(conv_8_4),
    aes(x = f4_count, y = reorder(Conf, f4_count)),
    fill = "deepskyblue3",
    alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    data = conversion_data |> drop_na(conv_8_4),
    aes(x = f4_count, y = reorder(Conf, f4_count),
    label = paste0(round(conv_8_4, 1), "%")), hjust = 1.2, color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1, 2)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Elite Eight to Final Four",
    x = "# of Teams Advanced",
    y = "") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )



(conv1 | conv2) / (conv3 | conv4) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "The Big Ten Had the Best Overall Conversion Rates",
    subtitle = "The conference never dropped below a 50% advancement rate at any stage.\n(Percentages = round-to-round win rate).",
    caption = "By: Jake Ostdiek | Source: SportsDataverse for R & SportsReference",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"))
  )









The conversion rates and number of teams advanced tells the final, undeniable piece of the story. While the SEC, Big 12, and others saw their representation fall off in the opening weekend, the Big Ten stayed true to what they knew, and won. From the Round of 64 to the Sweet 16, the Big Ten advanced 66.67% of their teams successfully while the SEC advanced 40% and the Big 12 advanced 37.5%. The Big Ten added a cherry on top and broke a conference record for number of teams that advanced to the Sweet 16, with 6 teams advancing (previously 4).
The success doesn’t stop there. From the Sweet 16 to the Elite Eight, the Big Ten was the only conference to advance more than one team, and they advanced four, breaking yet another conference record for number of teams in the Elite Eight (previously three). The high percentage of Big Ten teams surviving the early rounds of chaos proves that their regular season metrics weren’t by chance. They were prepared for the battlefield that is March Madness, because their entire season trained them to show up when it mattered most.
The Big Ten’s Final Statement: The Championship
You can have the best point differentials and the highest SOS, but without a banner to raise, the argument quickly falls apart. The Big Ten answered that with an exclamation point, with Michigan ultimately cutting down the nets to win it all.
The 2025-2026 season wasn’t up for debate. From the opening tip-off of the non-conference schedule, to the final buzzer of the National Championship game, the data tells the exact same story basketball fans watched all season. The Big Ten is the king of college basketball.

title: About
text: Hello! I am Jacob (Jake). I am a Data Science student driven by a passion for analyzing data to improve performance and achieve goals within the sports industry. My experience as a Sales Intern at Hudl has provided me with the ability to extract insights from data mining and taught me technical tools like Salesforce and SalesLoft, while my time with Husker Athletics has honed my skills in customer service and collaborative project management. My background is built on attention to detail, teamwork, and the ability to adapt to new technologies. I am actively looking to apply my analytical, sports-focused, and personable skills to a professional sports organization, to contribute to its success and growth.
